experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 2 are evil, so 0 and 3 is a clean-looking quest 1 team from my perspective. In team voting, good should generally approve a strong first team that excludes known evil. Approving reveals little beyond normal support for a plausible team and helps get a likely successful quest through.
Player 1: I know Player 2 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3] contains no known evil and is likely all good. As evil in round 1, the optimal play is usually to reject a clean-looking team to avoid an easy good pass, though an approve could create cover; since this team excludes both evils, rejecting is better. My vote only slightly reveals suspicion of this team, which is acceptable and helps evil if enough players also reject.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so team [0,3] contains no evil players. As evil, the optimal play is usually to avoid openly rejecting a clean-looking first team if that could create suspicion; approving can help me blend in while giving good a chance to pass quest 1, which is acceptable early and may build trust on uninvolved players. My vote reveals alignment-related preferences, so approving this two-good team is the less suspicious option.
Player 3: No role-specific information yet from prior play; as a Servant on round 1, quest 1, the standard good play is usually to approve an initial 2-person team unless there is evidence against it. I proposed [0,3], and I know I am good, so this team has at least one good member. Approving gives early information from the quest result without giving away anything extra beyond normal voting patterns.
Player 4: No hard role info yet from quest history or voting patterns. As Good Servant on round 1, a standard heuristic in 5-player Avalon is to approve the first team to gather information unless there's a strong reason not to. Team [0,3] includes the leader but not me; approving gives us an early signal from quest result without revealing special information, and a reject now is low-information.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 2 are evil. The proposed team is 0,3,4, which contains none of the known evil players and keeps player 0 and 3 from the successful first quest while adding 4, who is currently unconfirmed but preferable to including known evil. In team voting, good should approve teams that exclude known evil, and approving does not reveal too much beyond supporting a strong good-leaning team.
Player 1: I know 2 is my evil teammate, so 0/3/4 are all good. Last quest succeeded with 0 and 3, confirming no evil on that 2-person team. Current phase is team voting; as evil, I want to reject an all-good team that is likely to succeed and give Good a 2-0 lead. My vote reveals little since a reject on a strong-looking team can be rationalized, and stopping this team helps Evil more than blending in with an approve.
Player 2: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 1. Team [0,3,4] contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and move Good to 2 successes. As Evil, optimal play is to reject clean good teams, especially after [0,3] already succeeded and these players will look trusted. Rejecting gives little extra info because evil commonly opposes strong good teams; approving would help Good too much.
Player 3: Quest 1 team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and 3 look somewhat good, though a single success doesn't clear 0 completely. Player 4 now adds themself to that successful pair; with little evidence against 4, this is a reasonable exploratory team for quest 2. As a Good Servant in team voting, I want to approve solid teams that include a prior success core rather than reject without cause. Approving reveals I'm comfortable building from a successful team, which is fine for Good.
Player 4: Players 0 and 3 were on the successful first quest, which weakly suggests both could be good, though one evil could have passed. As a Servant, I want to approve teams that keep successful members and add myself for more good control. Team [0,3,4] is a strong low-risk follow-up from the prior success. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with backing a successful team, which helps good more than evil here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know players 1 and 2 are Evil, while 3 and 4 are not seen as Evil from Merlin perspective. Both prior successful teams included 0 and 3, with [0,3,4] also succeeding, so [0,3] is a very strong clean team for quest 3. As Good Merlin, optimal play is to approve a highly trusted team that excludes known Evil. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with wanting proven-good teams, and helps secure a likely third success.
Player 1: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and neither 0 nor 3 is on my evil team, so this proposed team [0,3] is all-good from my perspective and very likely to succeed again. As evil on quest 3 with good already up 2-0, the optimal play is to stop a clean good team from being approved if possible, so I should vote reject. My reject gives little away because some good players should also be suspicious of a repeated 2-person core being proposed for quest 3; approving would help good lock in a likely success.
Player 2: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,3] contains no evil players and would likely succeed again, moving Good to 3-0. With two clean successes already involving 0 and 3 repeatedly, they look trusted by the table; approving this only strengthens Good. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject any all-good team when possible, especially on quest 3. My reject gives little extra information since evil can plausibly object to repeated exclusion or to an uninformative 2-person team.
Player 3: I’m Good and only know public info. Teams [0,3] and [0,3,4] both succeeded with 0 fails, so 0, 3, and likely 4 look relatively good; 1 and 2 are less tested. Current phase is team voting on quest 3. Proposed team [0,3] repeats two players from prior successes but omits a third member on a 3-player quest, which is invalid/suspicious under normal Avalon structure, but given the prompt asks only approve/reject, the safest public-info choice is to approve a team centered on proven successful players. Approving reveals consistency with trusting successful teams and does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 4: Two successful quests give strongest trust to players 0, 3, and me (4), since [0,3] succeeded and [0,3,4] also succeeded with zero fails. As a Good Servant, optimal play is to reject this 2-player team for quest 3 because quest 3 should have 3 members; the proposed [0,3] omits me from the fully cleared trio and is not a valid/optimal continuation from the available trusted pool. Rejecting signals preference for the all-success core [0,3,4] and avoids endorsing a narrower team when we already have a better proven option.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 2 is evil. The proposed team [0,2] includes a known evil, so as Good I should reject to avoid handing Evil a fail opportunity on quest 3. Rejecting also aligns with prior successful info from teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], and does not reveal extra information beyond normal skepticism of a bad team.
Player 1: I know player 2 is evil with me. Team [0,2] includes one evil and excludes the highly trusted pair around 3/4; previous successful teams [0,3] and [0,3,4] make 0 and 3 look good, and likely 4 as well. In team voting as evil down 0-2, the optimal play is to approve a team containing my teammate so evil has a chance to fail quest 3 and avoid going to match point for good. Approving also looks consistent with being leader of this proposal and does not reveal extra information beyond supporting a plausible team.
Player 2: I know player 1 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,2] contains exactly one evil (me). Two prior quests succeeded with teams [0,3] and [0,3,4], so 0/3/4 look relatively good; player 1 was not on those teams. As evil on quest 3, we need to start creating failures, but in voting I should consider whether approving helps us. Approving puts me on a 2-person team where I can fail and make the game 2-1, which is strong for evil. My vote reveals little because evil can plausibly approve a team including themselves, and good player 1 as proposer may also be read in multiple ways. Best play is to approve and then likely fail if the team goes.
Player 3: Two successful quests involved player 0 and me (3), so both 0 and 3 look relatively good. Quest 2 with team 0,3,4 also succeeded, which mildly clears 4 or suggests evils avoided failing, but 2 and 1 are less tested. On quest 3 with a 2-person team, a single fail would lose it, so as Good I should prefer the strongest-cleared pair. Proposed team 0,2 drops me for largely untested 2, which is riskier than necessary after two clean successes with 0,3. Rejecting signals preference for proven-good teams and avoids giving an unvetted player a chance to sabotage.
Player 4: Two successful quests so far: [0,3] succeeded and [0,3,4] succeeded with no fails, which strongly suggests 0,3,4 are good. Player 2 is less confirmed, and swapping out confirmed 3 for untested 2 on quest 3 adds unnecessary risk when Good is up 2-0. As a Servant, optimal play is to reject teams that exclude stronger confirmed goods in favor of less-cleared players. My vote signals preference for consistency with proven success and may help avoid giving evil an opening.
